title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Scdoris is special, but so are other mushers Published: March 27th, 2005 Last Modified: March 27th, 2005 at 04:30 AM MANITOBA MOUNTAIN -- In the midst of making small talk with strangers while waiting on ski buddies after a glorious day of cutting up snow high in the Kenai Mountains, conversation drifted to the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race. The male half of a friendly couple volunteered that he had been inspired by the performance of Oregon musher Rachael Scdoris. It was hard to know how to react. As a reporter, I have spent considerable time watching, talking to and reading about Scdoris over the course of the past two months. As a reporter, one is bound to be objective about all that is seen and heard. No reporter would ever be accused of unfairness praising Scdoris for ove…

title: Fly-tying class casts off the preseason blues
summary: They are tiny works of art to be admired, but they have a purpose: to imitate nature and fool fish. And therein lies the appeal of tying feather and hair onto sharpened hooks -- it's particularly satisfying to catch fish on something you created. Tying flies is a Zen experience for some, a fun, calming craft to pass the winter and spark thoughts of warmer days, feeding trout and feisty salmon. "It's a nice thing to do when you're sitting around the house and it's 30 below," said Tom Cloninger of Three Rivers Fly and Tackle shop in Wasilla. "You don't feel so bad about watching TV if you're tying flies." Cloninger teaches a fly-tying class at Three Rivers Fly & Tackle in Wasilla, and several nights last week he taught a group of seven how to tie some of Alaska's most popular patterns: Iliam…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Winter king fishing out of Homer pays off to tune of $21,819 TOURNAMENT: 35.32-pound lunker wins annual event. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: March 27th, 2005 Last Modified: March 27th, 2005 at 04:30 AM When Keith Kline reeled in the 35-pound king salmon from Kachemak Bay earlier this month, he thought it might have a chance at the top prize in the Homer Chamber of Commerce's 12th annual winter king salmon tournament. But then other fishermen came by in his boat and said they'd caught one that was bigger. "One guy said, 'I got a 43-pounder here,' and I told Keith, 'Don't worry about it because I think they're lying,' " said Bernie Kline, Keith's father and captain of the boat Vixen. Turns out, Bernie Kline was right. Keith Kline won the derby March 19 with his 35.32-po…

title: City students take cultural leap to remote village
summary: JUNEAU -- Five Juneau-Douglas High School students recently got a taste of ice fishing, mushing, trapping, muktuk and beaver tail in Russian Mission, a Yup'ik Eskimo village on the Yukon River near the coast. The weeklong experience in mid-February was federally funded through the Rose Urban Rural Exchange of the Alaska Humanities Forum. Since the program began five years ago, about 300 students from cities and villages in Alaska have visited each other, said program director Panu Lucier in Anchorage. An exchange between students from Floyd Dryden Middle School and Napakiak also is taking place this year. In mid-April, students from Russian Mission -- a village of 330 people 375 miles west of Anchorage -- will visit Juneau, where they will experience bowling, movies, malls and perhaps walk…

summary: Board revises Nelchina proposal CARIBOU: Overhaul of hunting rules will be given more thought. By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News Published: March 10th, 2005 Last Modified: March 10th, 2005 at 02:24 AM One of Alaska's most intractable hunting issues -- who should be allowed to hunt the 36,700 caribou roaming the Nelchina and Copper river basins -- was stalked by the Alaska Board of Game this week through a thicket of sport-hunting and local subsistence interests. In the end, the board eased off the trigger. After spending Monday and Tuesday in a lively discussion with Native leaders and hunting representatives, the seven-member board voted to revise a proposal to overhaul the hunting rules, with the idea that people in villages and Alaska's cities could study it further. The board could …

title: 3 of 3 A skier passes posted signs on the race course. This year's Oosik Classic almost didn't happen. Three straight da…
summary: 3 of 3 A skier passes posted signs on the race course. This year's Oosik Classic almost didn't happen. Three straight days of rain last week left trails icy, but officials decided by Wednesday that the race would go on as planned. The party after the laid-back competition is just as important as the race itself. "Every single vendor in Talkeetna all worked together to do the banquet," said Arthur Mannix, a race organizer. Photo by Ron Wilmot / Anchorage Daily News

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: BIRDING Audubon group plans Middleton Island trip Join Anchorage Audubon on April 30 for an opportunity to visit Middleton Island, where more than 175 species of birds have been spotted. The island is characterized as a migrant trap because of its distance from the mainland. Nesting cormorants, kittiwakes, murres, auklets and puffins are on the island, as well as rarities such as gray-tailed tattlers, bristle-thighed curlews and chestnut-sided warblers. Anchorage Audubon has chartered an aircraft from Anchorage for one day of birding and sightseeing. A University of Alaska Fairbanks graduate student studying black oystercatchers will talk about his study and show participants around the island. The cost is $225 per person, which includes transportation to and from Middleton Island in a Sup…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: 'Poetry in motion' at Telemark festival Telemark festival honors a skier's love of a sport rich in athleticism and in beauty By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: March 20th, 2005 Last Modified: March 20th, 2005 at 06:43 AM Tim Charnon zigzagged gracefully across the finish line, a smile on his face. There was no tension in this race. Skiers had put on skins, raced up Mount Alyeska, skate-skied across the ridge and then descended in telltale telemark lunges. No one knew who'd come in first -- and no one cared. The second annual Jeff Nissman Telepalooza telemark festival was a weekend of remembrance, not results. It was a time for backcountry skiers to showcase their sport while celebrating the life of a departed friend. Nissman died in January 2004 when a giant slab of ice sl…

summary: Officers meet over trail head thefts SMASH-AND-GRABS: Rangers, police gather to discuss possible solutions to a growing problem. By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News Published: March 18th, 2005 Last Modified: March 18th, 2005 at 04:12 AM Police, park rangers and city officials met this week to brainstorm ways to combat a long-standing problem that spikes each spring and summer around Anchorage: trail head thefts. Smashed car windows and stolen wallets usually don't elicit the same police response as more serious crimes, such as burglary and assault. But even those who put the calls low on the priority list say the trail head episodes, which are on the rise, can be extremely frustrating for victims. People go out to enjoy the wilderness and instead are met with a paperwork nightmare and…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Elbow in for tube ride at Alyeska park CROWDED: Despite shorter sessions, demand for fast ride and tow-rope lift is soaring. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: March 13th, 2005 Last Modified: March 13th, 2005 at 01:24 AM When Andy Rembert tried to redeem the Glacier Tubing Park passes his family received as part of their lodging at Alyeska Resort in late January, he was told the park was full until late in the day. The family had planned to get back to their Chugiak home by late afternoon but instead had to wait until 3 p.m. to take advantage of the park's last opening, putting them back home well after dark. Rembert's story is a familiar one. Since the opening of Glacier Tubing Park in January, it has been an unprecedented hit with snow lovers. The two-lane tubing hill of…

title: Elbow in for tube ride at Alyeska park
summary: When Andy Rembert tried to redeem the Glacier Tubing Park passes his family received as part of their lodging at Alyeska Resort in late January, he was told the park was full until late in the day. The family had planned to get back to their Chugiak home by late afternoon but instead had to wait until 3 p.m. to take advantage of the park's last opening, putting them back home well after dark. Rembert's story is a familiar one. Since the opening of Glacier Tubing Park in January, it has been an unprecedented hit with snow lovers. The two-lane tubing hill offers sledders a fast but short ride down, accompanied by a tow-rope-assisted pull back up. It's an activity that anyone can do, and it requires no special skills other than holding on tight. Gary Scott, marketing manager for Alyeska, said…

title: Men wait to cross Strait
summary: WALES -- Two men who met at the bottom of the world are in another frigid corner of it today, attempting to cross the partly frozen Bering Strait. Dixie Dansercoer, a Belgian who makes a living from extreme athletic feats, and Alaskan Troy Henkels, a telephone company troubleshooter, say they will walk, ski, sail, kite and even swim to get across the moving water and colliding ice floes of the strait. "Human beings, they're strivers," Dansercoer said Friday by telephone from Nome. "We're probably the next generation of all those pioneers that have done the same thing. It's a good tradition." Dansercoer, a father of four, lives in Huldenburg, Belgium. Henkels is from Eagle River. They are not the first to expose themselves to ripping currents and polar bears in the pursuit of adventure. Sin…

summary: Field Notes Published: March 13th, 2005 Last Modified: March 13th, 2005 at 01:24 AM SPRING BREAK Trailside Discovery offers camp Trailside Discovery is offering a Winter Wilderness Camp Monday through Friday at Spring Creek Farm in Palmer. Programs will emphasize hands-on outdoor activities such as winter survival, snowshoeing, building snow shelters, orienteering and winter birding. Trailside Discovery is an American Camp Association-accredited program. The camp is $30 per day or $140 for all five days. Call 274-5437 or visit www.akcenter.org . Check out spring break ski camps Children in the Anchorage School District can use spring break to fine-tune their ski skills at Alyeska. The resort offers a "Mini Silvertip Camp" for those ages 5 to 10, from 10:30 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. March 21-23. Th…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: March 13th, 2005 Last Modified: March 13th, 2005 at 04:59 AM ASTRONOMY Learn about meteorites at Nature Center program Learn about meteorites at 7 p.m. Friday at the Eagle River Nature Center. An amateur astronomer will lead an indoor program about meteorites for 60 to 90 minutes. Afterward, if skies are clear, the program will move to the outside decks to view the night sky. Bring binoculars or a spotting scope to share. Because of space constraints, groups of 10 or more must call in advance to make arrangements for a class. The program is free; parking is $5 a vehicle. For details, call 694-2108. BIRDING Audubon group plans Middleton Island trip Join Anchorage Audubon on April 30 for an opportunity to visit Middleton Island, where more than 175 species of bir…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: February 22nd, 2004 Last Modified: February 29th, 2004 at 01:05 AM BOATING Hone your kayaking skills in two locations Alaska Kayak Academy is offering beginner and intermediate kayak classes Sunday afternoons at the YMCA in Anchorage and Saturday mornings at Wasilla High School. Call 1-877-215-6600 or 1-907-746-6600 for information. Also coming up from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. March 13 at the Bartlett High School pool will be the academy's Cabin Fever Paddle Fest 2004. There will be events for all skill levels and types of kayakers, from touring to white water. Go to www.Alaskakayakacademy.com to learn more. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Denali Park can party in winter FESTIVAL: Visitors and locals celebrate. By ELIZABETH MANNING Special to the Daily News Published: March 6th, 2005 Last Modified: March 6th, 2005 at 01:48 AM DENALI NATIONAL PARK -- Bears, buses and bustle. Those are a few common images that come to mind when Alaskans think of Denali National Park and Preserve. That's summer, though. Winter couldn't be more different. Winter is the park's quiet season -- intimate and unhurried. It's also the rare time when locals outnumber visitors in the park's nearby communities. Last weekend our family ventured south from Fairbanks for Denali National Park's annual Winterfest. Driving to the park entrance was like coming into a ghost town. Businesses all seemed closed and hotels and shops were boarded up. Inside the park,…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sleepless in the snow, Sorlie takes the lead STEVE WILSTEIN COMMENT Published: March 9th, 2005 Last Modified: March 9th, 2005 at 05:16 AM NIKOLAI -- Dogs, not mushers, are tested for steroids, stimulants and other performance-enhancing drugs in the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race. That says something about which ones are the real athletes in this 1,100-mile journey. These knee-high Alaska huskies and other mixed breeds, weighing on average about 45 pounds, run silently and open-mouthed over frozen rivers, lakes and mountains. They bark only when they know they're getting ready to leave a checkpoint, showing their eagerness to get back on the trail. After reaching the next checkpoint, they curl down onto beds of straw scattered by their mushers, then eat and snooze, even when surrounded by a d…

summary: Rookie mushers just want to make it to the burled arch in Nome Idtarod XXXIII By JOEL GAY Anchorage Daily News Published: March 9th, 2005 Last Modified: March 9th, 2005 at 05:16 AM PUNTILLA LAKE -- At night, the dog-team parking lot at this checkpoint glowed like a busy little village, lit by the meandering headlamps of mushers, veterinarians and checkpoint officials. By morning, the lot was nearly deserted, which was fine with Andy Letzring, a 21-year-old first-time racer from McMillan, Mich. "I've been trying to run by myself," he said as his team slept in the now-quiet yard and he prepared to bed down too. "It takes away from your focus" to have too many distractions, he said. For Jeff Holt, a North Pole musher who's also on his first Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race, the advantage of runni…

title: Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News Local entrepreneurs are moving to take advantage of the opportunities they for…
summary: Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News Local entrepreneurs are moving to take advantage of the opportunities they foresee in Whittier as a result of next May's return of the cruise-ship industry. The Inn at Whittier, a 25-room, New England-style hotel scheduled to open in September, is one of several multimillion-dollar tourism projects on the horizon. Work on the inn, near the entrance to the boat harbor, continued Thursday as kayakers returned to port.

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: DOCUMENTARY Award-winning film "Being Caribou" shows at museum The Sierra Club, Alaska chapter, and the Anchorage Museum of History and Art offer two showings of the award-winning documentary "Being Caribou," 5:30 and 7:30 p.m. Tuesday at the museum, 121 W. Seventh Ave. The film has been shown at the Calgary and Banff film festivals and was winner of the AGC People's Choice Award. It follows two Canadians, who on April 8, 2003, left the remote community of Old Crow, Yukon Territory, to join the Porcupine Caribou herd on its life journey. For five months, Karsten Heuer and Leanne Allison migrated on foot with the 123,000-member caribou herd from wintering grounds to calving grounds in Alaska's Arctic National Wildlife Refuge and back again. This documentary explores the debate for oil and e…

summary: On the wings of spring Southcentral shorebird festivals a rite of the season Stories by ELIZABETH MANNING Anchorage Daily News Published: April 11th, 2004 Last Modified: April 18th, 2004 at 12:47 AM Kelley Weaverling, the owner of Orca Book and Sound Co. in Cordova, has a favorite spring ritual: He takes a stool and thermos onto the mud flats of the Copper River Delta, then sits down to wait. Inevitably, huge flocks of shorebirds -- sometimes 10,000 or more -- swirl above him. Surrounded by the sound of thousands of wings beating, Weaverling watches as birds dart about in dense clouds, undisturbed by his presence. Their tiny bodies flash white, then dark as they spin in unison in between feeding on invertebrates and fly larvae in the mud. "It's just too cool," he said. "It's kind of like w…

summary: Tesoro Iron Dog Snowmachine Race: Former U.S. Olympic team member turns to a different kind of ski By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: February 12th, 2005 Last Modified: February 13th, 2005 at 03:55 AM The trail from Olympic Stadium to Alaska Iron Dog podium is so long and difficult that Sacha Gros knows he may never complete the journey. When the former U.S. Olympic Team skier talks of those who have seized the choicest prize in Alaska motor sports, there's a hint of envy in his voice. Most Iron Dog contenders literally grew up on snowmachines. Tyson Johnson, the runner-up in 1997, used to ride from his family's Yentna River cabin to high school in Skwentna every day, a distance of about 15 miles. "One time he made it in eight minutes,'' Gros said. Over the years and the miles…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Wolf-kill efforts proceed for now JUDGE: Request denied to halt program until May trial. By JOEL GAY Anchorage Daily News Published: February 3rd, 2005 Last Modified: February 3rd, 2005 at 02:12 AM An Anchorage judge on Wednesday upheld the state's controversial aerial wolf-kill programs, paving the way for private pilots to shoot more than 600 wolves this winter in rural Alaska. In her decision, Superior Court Judge Sharon Gleason refused to grant an injunction sought by the Connecticut-based group Friends of Animals and six Alaska residents to halt predator-control efforts until their lawsuit against the state goes to trial in May. The same group of plaintiffs sued the state in 2003, saying the Alaska Board of Game and Alaska Department of Fish and Game hadn't justified the predator-prog…

title: Seven-day license bill advances
summary: JUNEAU -- Alaska fishermen could give tourists a glimpse of life on a commercial fishing boat if legislators approve a seven-day crew license bill. But the sponsor, Rep. Carl Moses, D-Unalaska, may object to a version passed out of committee this week that would prevent people from receiving pay for their labor. The House Resources Committee's version of the bill moved out of committee Monday. People would pay $30 for the temporary license. Rep. Paul Seaton, R-Homer, said the revision was needed to keep commercial fishing operations from using the bill to hire cheap labor. Moses and at least one fishermen's group do not like the committee's revision. They had hoped that commercial fishing boat skippers could use the short-term license to temporarily fill spots on their crew during peak sea…

title: Mystery surrounds outdoorsman's death
summary: No one will ever know for certain what happened to former Palmer resident Bart Schleyer. His last contact with another human was when a chartered floatplane left him at the larger of the Reid Lakes in Canada's Yukon Territory on Sept. 14. When the plane returned two weeks later, the experienced woodsman was gone. The Royal Canadian Mounted Police promptly launched a search. It found nothing. The RCMP left. Across the great white silence of the Yukon and Alaska, there is a rich history of people wandering into the wilderness never to return. Thirty-five-year-old Texan Carl McCunn flew into a remote valley in the Brooks Range in 1981 with a dream of camping out for the summer photographing the wilderness. A diary found near his frozen body in February 1982 detailed how the plane that was to …

title: Nelchina hunting reforms on table
summary: State game managers are considering a radical overhaul of the Nelchina caribou hunt, one of the most popular yet problematic freezer-fillers in Alaska, and in the process they may redefine subsistence hunting in the 21st century. If the Alaska Board of Game approves Proposal 155 next month, any state resident will be able to subsistence hunt in areas north and east of Anchorage for caribou and moose. It would be the first time in 15 years the highway-accessible hunt had such unlimited access. But there's a catch -- several, actually -- designed to make the new hunt adhere more closely to popular ideas of subsistence. Those who signed up couldn't hunt or trap elsewhere in Alaska that year. They would have to salvage virtually every ounce of meat. And they couldn't use airplanes or motor hom…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Wood bison revival Biologist hopes to restore a living relic of Alaska's past By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: March 6th, 2005 Last Modified: March 6th, 2005 at 01:48 AM FAIRBANKS -- Bob Stephenson sat at a table in his Alaska Department of Fish and Game office surrounded by the graying bones and curled horns of wood bison that tell the story of their evolution. The bones are as thick as the base of a wine bottle. Stephenson, Fort Yukon area wildlife biologist, can recall minute details about each of them -- where they were found, how old they are and what stage of evolution the creature that once owned them came from. For more than a decade, Stephenson has been a champion of the wood bison, a long, rangy grazing animal that disappeared from Alaska about 200 years ago bu…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Proposed city park features skate oval MIDTOWN: Committee approves plans, but project still faces some hurdles. By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News Published: March 3rd, 2005 Last Modified: March 3rd, 2005 at 02:48 AM If the many interests can agree, Cuddy Family Midtown Park will become home to an Olympic-sized speedskating oval. It will also feature a pond, a plaza, a hilly trail and an amphitheater. And it may forge a special relationship with the library, with parking reconfigured so that a walkway leads from the library fountain into the park. The new plan for this scenic but under-used piece of open space next to Loussac Library advanced Wednesday when a city committee adopted it. "This park will be wildly popular," predicted Art Geuss, a former city parks commissioner and one…

title: Craig Medred: Has friendly, little REI grown a bit too big for its britches?
summary: When kayaker Andy Ryan in Seattle -- REI member 348240 -- got the runaround from America's largest consumer cooperative, climber Neil O'Donnell in Anchorage -- REI member 1228171 -- got mad. And therein lies a story, but first a little background. As many know, REI is the acronym that grew into the common name for one of America's largest outdoor retailers. The company started in 1938 as Recreational Equipment Inc., a Seattle-based consumer cooperative. On paper, it remains a cooperative. And in its self-promotion, REI makes almost as big a deal of this as it does of its social consciousness and environmental awareness. "Being a cooperative business remains central to REI,'' the company proclaims on its Web site. www.rei.com . "While nonmembers are welcome to shop at REI, only members enjo…

title: History, morality come with fries at the Arctic Roadrunner
summary: Richard Dick Sanchis didn't set out to become a grill-cook-turned-philosopher-turned-historian. Things just sort of happened.
